---
title: "Surveys"
description: "A curated map of surveys, course-style overviews, and broad entry points that help readers move from stable math into active literature without drowning in papers."
page-type: research-direction
status: published
update-status: monitor
level: research
topic: surveys
prerequisites:
  - /research/index.qmd
  - /research/theorem-families.qmd
  - /research/directions.qmd
  - /paper-lab/reading-trails.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Choose the right kind of survey for your current background and goal."
  - "Separate textbook-scale, course-scale, and frontier-scale survey material."
  - "Use surveys as entry points instead of turning them into another unread backlog."
  - "Map major research directions in the site to strong external survey resources."
applications:
  - literature-entry
  - machine-learning-theory
  - graph-learning
  - optimization
  - generative-modeling
keywords:
  - surveys
  - literature map
  - tutorials
  - course notes
  - entry points
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: quarterly
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
stable-sources:
  - "Research index"
  - "Theorem Families"
  - "Directions"
current-sources:
  - "CS229T course description"
  - "CS224W 2024"
  - "High-Dimensional Probability course"
  - "Flow Matching Guide and Code"
---

## Why This Page

Surveys are useful, but they are easy to misuse.

People often grab a survey hoping it will remove all difficulty. Instead, a good survey usually does something narrower and more valuable:

- gives vocabulary
- organizes a literature patch
- shows how results fit together
- tells you what is stable and what is still moving

This page is here to help you pick the right survey for the right job.

## Survey Snapshot

- `Type`: top-level research map
- `Setting`: readers who want an entry point into a literature area without starting from random recent papers
- `Main claim`: the best survey depends on whether you need `backbone`, `bridge`, or `frontier context`
- `Why it matters`: surveys save time only when they are chosen with a purpose

## Three Kinds Of Surveys

### 1. Textbook-scale survey

This is what you read when the field still feels mathematically unstable to you.

Use it for:

- definitions
- standard objects
- proof backbones
- historical shape of the area

Examples:

- a full book
- lecture notes that function like a book
- a mature graduate course

### 2. Course-scale survey

This is often the best starting point for research readers.

Use it for:

- a structured reading path
- topic selection with some curation already done
- a bridge from math to modern applications

Examples:

- a course homepage with reading list
- course notes
- official lecture schedule with curated references

### 3. Frontier survey

This is what you read when you already know the basics and want a snapshot of the active literature.

Use it for:

- recent variants
- terminology changes
- open problems
- where the area has moved in the last few years

Examples:

- recent survey papers
- living tutorials
- current course pages tied to active research groups

## How To Choose A Survey

Ask one question first:

`What is my current bottleneck?`

If the bottleneck is:

- missing math backbone -> start with `textbook-scale`
- missing big picture -> start with `course-scale`
- missing frontier map -> start with `frontier survey`

Do not ask a frontier survey to do the job of a textbook.

## How To Read A Survey Without Drowning

Use this rule:

1. read the abstract, introduction, and conclusion
2. locate the section that matches your direction
3. extract a `small reading trail`
4. stop and read one anchor paper before continuing

A survey is not a moral obligation to read from page 1 to page 80.

## Survey Entry Point 1: High-Dimensional Probability

### Best use

Choose this when concentration, random matrices, and non-asymptotic bounds keep appearing in papers you care about.

### Good starting sources

- [High-Dimensional Probability course](https://www.math.uci.edu/~rvershyn/teaching/hdp/hdp.html)
- [High-Dimensional Probability, 2nd edition](https://www.math.uci.edu/~rvershyn/papers/HDP-book/HDP-book.html?source=content_type%3Areact%7Cfirst_level_url%3Aarticle%7Csection%3Amain_content%7Cbutton%3Abody_link)
- [Four lectures on probabilistic methods for data science](https://www.math.uci.edu/~rvershyn/papers/four-lectures-probability-data.pdf)

### Why this works

This direction benefits from having:

- one course-scale entry point
- one book-scale backbone
- one shorter bridge text

### Internal trail

1. [Concentration and Common Inequalities](../topics/foundation/probability/concepts/concentration-and-common-inequalities.qmd)
2. [Theorem Families](theorem-families.qmd)
3. one of the sources above

## Survey Entry Point 2: Convex Optimization And Solver Structure

### Best use

Choose this when your papers keep using:

- convexity
- certificates
- duality
- constrained optimization
- solver layers

### Good starting sources

- [EE364a: Convex Optimization I](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364a/index.html)
- [Stanford Engineering Everywhere: EE364A](https://see.stanford.edu/Course/EE364A)
- [Boyd teaching page](https://stanford.edu/~boyd/teaching.html)

### Why this works

Optimization is a field where the best survey is often a course rather than a standalone paper.

### Internal trail

1. [Optimization](../topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd)
2. [Duality and Certificates](../topics/advanced/optimization/concepts/duality-and-certificates.qmd)
3. [Optimization for Machine Learning](../applications/machine-learning/optimization-for-machine-learning.qmd)
4. one of the sources above

## Survey Entry Point 3: Graph Learning And Geometric Deep Learning

### Best use

Choose this when you want a broad map of:

- graph neural networks
- representation learning on graphs
- homophily / heterophily
- spectral and geometric viewpoints

### Good starting sources

- [CS224W 2024](https://snap.stanford.edu/class/cs224w-2024/)
- [Geometric Deep Learning: Grids, Groups, Graphs, Geodesics, and Gauges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.13478)

### Why this works

This area moves fast, so pairing one current course hub with one broad survey is often better than reading many isolated papers first.

### Internal trail

1. [Eigenvalues and Diagonalization](../topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/eigenvalues-and-diagonalization.qmd)
2. [Graph Rewiring, Homophily, and Heterophily](../applications/machine-learning/graph-rewiring-homophily-and-heterophily.qmd)
3. [Long-Range Dependence and Oversquashing in Graphs](../applications/machine-learning/long-range-dependence-and-oversquashing-in-graphs.qmd)
4. one of the sources above

## Survey Entry Point 4: Modern Learning Theory

### Best use

Choose this when your questions revolve around:

- generalization
- implicit bias
- overparameterization
- distribution shift
- theory for large nonlinear models

### Good starting sources

- [CS229T Course Description](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2136161)
- [CS229T/STAT231 Notes](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs229t/notes.pdf)

### Why this works

Learning theory is broad enough that a course-scale map is often more useful than one giant survey paper.

### Internal trail

1. [Generalization, Overfitting, and Validation](../applications/machine-learning/generalization-overfitting-and-validation.qmd)
2. [Regularization, Implicit Bias, and Model Complexity](../applications/machine-learning/regularization-implicit-bias-and-model-complexity.qmd)
3. [Directions](directions.qmd)
4. one of the sources above

## Survey Entry Point 5: Diffusion, Score, And Flow Models

### Best use

Choose this when you want a wider map around:

- score-based generation
- diffusion models
- flow matching
- transport views of generation

### Good starting sources

- [Stanford HAI: What are Diffusion Models?](https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-definitions/what-are-diffusion-models)
- [Flow Matching Guide and Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.06264)

### Why this works

This area changes quickly, so a short conceptual overview plus a current guide is usually a better opening than a stack of recent papers.

### Internal trail

1. [Diffusion Models and Denoising](../applications/machine-learning/diffusion-models-and-denoising.qmd)
2. [Score Matching and the SDE View of Diffusion](../applications/machine-learning/score-matching-and-the-sde-view-of-diffusion.qmd)
3. [Flow Matching and Transport Views of Generation](../applications/machine-learning/flow-matching-and-transport-views-of-generation.qmd)
4. one of the sources above

## Common Survey Mistakes

### Reading surveys too early

If the survey uses notation you still cannot translate, go back to topic pages or `Paper Lab`.

### Reading surveys too late

If you already understand the area, the survey may no longer be the best use of your time. Jump to a paper lab or current paper instead.

### Treating surveys as proof sources

Many surveys explain ideas well but compress proofs aggressively. They are often for structure, not full derivation.

### Turning one survey into a new unread backlog

The point is to extract a trail, not to collect a dozen references you never return to.

## What To Learn Next

- [Venues](venues.qmd), if you want to understand where the literature you just entered tends to live
- [Paper Lab](../paper-lab/index.qmd), if you want paper-reading workflow instead of literature entry
- [Directions](directions.qmd), if you want to choose a frontier before choosing a survey

## Sources And Further Reading

- [High-Dimensional Probability course](https://www.math.uci.edu/~rvershyn/teaching/hdp/hdp.html) - `Second pass` - one of the cleanest current course-scale entry points into modern concentration and random-matrix ideas. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [High-Dimensional Probability, 2nd edition](https://www.math.uci.edu/~rvershyn/papers/HDP-book/HDP-book.html?source=content_type%3Areact%7Cfirst_level_url%3Aarticle%7Csection%3Amain_content%7Cbutton%3Abody_link) - `First pass` - durable book-scale backbone for the probability side of current theory work. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [EE364a: Convex Optimization I](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364a/index.html) - `Second pass` - current official course-scale map for convexity, duality, and certificates. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [CS224W 2024](https://snap.stanford.edu/class/cs224w-2024/) - `Paper bridge` - current official hub for graph-learning readings and topic sequencing. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Geometric Deep Learning: Grids, Groups, Graphs, Geodesics, and Gauges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.13478) - `Paper bridge` - broad survey bridge into graph and geometric learning. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [CS229T Course Description](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2136161) - `Paper bridge` - concise current map of modern learning-theory topics. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Flow Matching Guide and Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.06264) - `Paper bridge` - useful current guide for the score/flow side of generative modeling. Checked `2026-04-25`.
